merchant_editorial:
Nestled in the buzzing Paddington Five Ways, Tequila Mockingbird first opened it’s restored doors on August 2016. You’ll find us in a heritage-listed, converted 1898 terrace. There are three completely unique and separate spaces to dine in, each with their own special character and atmosphere.

You might be surprised to know that there’s a whole lot more to Latin food than burritos and fajitas. It’s actually a wide range of food, all the way from Mexico to Argentina. A unique blend of Mayan, Aztec, Spanish, Portuguese, Japanese, Chinese, and Afro Caribbean flavours, Latin American food is diverse, unexpected, and most importantly, delicious!

merchant_editorial: Henrys Neutral Bay: Where Fusion Flavours Meet Fine Moments.Tucked away in the leafy, serene streets of Neutral Bay, Henrys is a hidden gem redefining North Shore dining with its unique and intriguing take on contemporary fusion. More than just a restaurant, Henrys is a celebration of flavour, design, and the simple joy of sharing good food with great company. From the moment guests step into Henrys, they’re met with a space that feels both elevated and warmly inviting — where sleek black, white, and gold interiors blend with natural textures to create a relaxed yet sophisticated atmosphere. It’s the kind of place where long lunches turn into unforgettable dinners, and every gathering feels like a special occasion. At the heart of Henrys is a bold, curated menu that brings together Asian and South American influences with a distinctly modern twist — a vision brought to life by the duo, Chef Jonathan Hitaka, a Japanese-Brazilian talent whose upbringing and training bridge the flavours of both continents and Chef Reina Jo from South Korea, whose refined technique and palate bring balance, nuance and elegance to every dish. “Our food tells a story about culture, contrast, and connection,” says Chef Hitaka. “We take the comfort of South American tradition and elevate it with Asian precision and creativity. Every dish is designed to surprise and satisfy.” Dishes are meant for sharing — from seared scallops with herb oil and caviar to crispy calamari tossed in togarashi and served with spicy habanero mayo. Mains like the crispy duck breast with sour cherry and Japanese jus or the signature Humpty Doo barramundi are both showstoppers and crowd-pleasers, effortlessly balancing flavour and finesse. Henrys also shines as an events venue. Whether it’s a birthday, engagement party, baby shower or corporate dinner, the team at Henrys works closely with guests to create memorable experiences. With options ranging from intimate private dining to full venue hire, events at Henrys feel effortless yet extraordinary. The set menus are a feast for the senses — featuring house favourites like salmon ceviche, lamb rump cap, and the now-iconic fried cauliflower with toasted sesame dressing. Every detail at Henrys is crafted with care—from the beautifully plated dishes to the attentive yet unobtrusive service and flexible event offerings. For those with dietary requirements, the kitchen team is happy to create alternatives that never compromise on taste or creativity. For anyone seeking a dining experience that’s as delicious as it is stylish, Henrys Neutral Bay delivers — every plate, every time.

merchant_editorial: Sedap offers an array of classic Malaysian street eats. From famous Penang hawker dishes such as Char Kuey Teow, a stir-fried flat rice noodle dish with prawns and Chinese sausages delivering a big smoky flavour, to Pai Kut Wong a Malaysian-style sweet and sour spare ribs and the more commonly known Nasi Goreng. title: 14% off

merchant_editorial:
Inspired by its many traditions and culinary practices, Zoya and Saba, aim to create an authentic “home” for Persian food in Australia. They invite others to revel in its delicate homemade production, the social connections that bind its experience, and its historical roots. Shila Kitchen is all about exploration, adventure, and the introduction of new experiences; thus, taking on the name of “Shila” – a fictional bird in One Thousand and One Nights (a collection of Middle Eastern folk tales) who would venture into far-off lands with a sailor named Sinbad.

Each dish is prepared with the utmost care and patience, slow cooked to the nest texture, aroma, and infusion of flavours. As such, Persian food often takes plenty of time and energy – though result in delicious creations well worth the wait. Fresh or dried, their cuisine’s love affair with herbs brings taste and character to an already colourful repertoire of dishes.

Gift a table today and taste a dish from 9000 years of cultivation and social togetherness in Balmain, in heart of Sydney.

merchant_editorial:
On paper, South Coast Distillery was established in 2017, but the story really began in 2011. In a share-house in Wollongong, a mutual appreciation for gin by all housemates, led to a tradition of “Martini Thursdays.” Each week, they explored all varieties of the spirit, hoping to discover the perfect gin for the ultimate martini. Seeking inspiration, they met with distillers far and wide to learn about the craft and focused on perfecting their own recipes. By 2018, they were primed to produce their first commercial release, Sublime Gin. The globally recognised product was named 2019 Champion Australian Small Batch Spirit at the prestigious Australian Distilled Spirits Awards and received the Silver Medal at the influential San Francisco World Spirits Competition. Recently, the prestigious 2020 Silver Award Contemporary Style Gin at the London World Gin Awards selecting the very best in all internationally recognised styles of gin.

Look out for an exciting line-up of new releases for 2022, including Damson Plum Gin, Smoked Gin, Illawarra Plum ‘Sloe Style’ Gin, and Pink Gin.

Our Cellar Door is now open every Friday, 4pm till late, and Saturdays Midday till late. Come and try our range of products, indulge in a cocktail or two and preview upcoming releases. You can also enjoy a selection of beers, wines and spirits made by other local producers.

We are also open for tours, group bookings, and gin making workshops by appointment, and takeaway sales during business hours during the week.

name: Vino e Cucina eGift Card
title: 14% off
subtitle: Enjoy 14% off
merchant_editorial:
Vino e Cucina is an Italian restaurant and bar in Paddington, at the hub of the Five Ways precinct, in Sydney’s Eastern Suburbs.

The European atmosphere is aided by alfresco dining on the street, as well as a more romantic setting out the back. There is also a modern dining setting inside this charming heritage terrace building, with the walls adorned with magnificent artwork by local artists.

Vino e Cucina is the creation of restaurateurs, chef Michael Bengtsson and Julia Walker-Bengtsson, a husband and wife team, who have also created other loved Five Ways Paddington venues Eat Thai and Tuk Tuk bar (formerly Heeley St Espresso).

‘Vino’ or ‘Vino e Cucina Paddo’, as it is also affectionately known, is becoming a favourite among locals and traditional Italian dining connoisseurs.

The warm Italian ambience is a result of meticulous attention to detail by these seasoned restaurateurs. The elegant dining room, relaxed terraza and alfresco tables out the front, have been furnished with a very personal touch. There are even pots of herbs growing in old imported Italian tomato cans in the terraza out the back and red geraniums in wine casks out the front where you can sit and watch Paddington go by.

The staff have all been carefully selected to represent Vino e Cucina as a provenziale Italian trattoria style restaurant, bar and pizzeria (there’s even a dedicated Pizzaiolo), but with a few modern touches. Couples can come for a romantic dinner or families can come and enjoy a hearty feast of pizza. Those wanting to dine solo, or just enjoy a glass of wine, are also very welcome. There is also a Salumi bar and a wine selection to keep you busy for hours, and we must give a special mention to the homemade Limoncello.

If you can’t make it to Italy for a holiday, this is the next best thing.

At Avido Ristorante we aim to serve simple and fresh Italian food in a family-friendly setting.

Avido is the brainchild of chef and owner Sammy Jeon, who discovered his passion for food after moving to Orange in 2009.

Sammy has since opened four restaurants in the premier food and wine region of central west NSW: Mr Sushi King, Orange (opened 2012); Mr Sushi King, Mudgee (2014); Mr Lim Korean/Asian fusion restaurant, Orange (2016); and Avido Ristorante, Orange (2019).

name: Berrima Vault House eGift Card
title: 14% off
subtitle: Enjoy 14% off
merchant_editorial:
Situated in heritage-listed Berrima, Australia’s best-preserved 1800s village, this unique building started life as Taylor’s Crown Inn, hand-built by convicts in 1844 using local sandstone.

Berrima was founded in 1831 with surveyor Robert Hoddle (later Melbourne’s planner) taking design cues from traditional English villages. This meant administrative buildings like the Berrima Gaol and Courthouse were built - along with 13 public houses to satisfy a growing thirst.

Taylor’s was one of those first pubs and was soon joined by the barracks, built to accommodate wardens from the gaol - what’s now known as the Southern Highland’s largest fireplace was where they grabbed some shut-eye.

Over the years Taylor’s Crown Inn has been a public house, private house, restaurant, art gallery, bakery and more, mirroring the changing fortunes of Berrima itself.

History shows that by the 1860s, Berrima was ready to take on a more significant role in the region, and the expectation was the rail line from Sydney to Goulburn would pass through the village bringing further growth. When that failed to materialise, the village contracted and became just another stop on the main road south.

The opening of the bypass in the late 1980s reinvigorated tourism to Berrima, an industry that thrives today, with the village recently winning the NSW Top Tourism Town award.

name: Catalina Restaurant eGift Card
title: 14% off
subtitle: Enjoy 14% off
merchant_editorial:
Catalina has been the archetypal family business since its inception in 1994. The late Michael McMahon and his wife Judy were both fixtures in the Sydney dining scene since its modern birth at Berowra Waters Inn, and established a dedication to excellence in their restaurants that their children James and Kate also emulate.

Michael and Judy began their careers with the late Len Evans (OBE) working at the legendary Bulletin Place. In 1978, they made the move to Gay and Tony Bilson's Berowra Waters Inn, Michael as floor manager and Judy as their first female waitress.

In 1983 Judy and Michael purchased Barrenjoey House in Palm Beach and worked with a young Neil Perry, subsequently opening Perry's in Paddington, both of which attained Two Hat ratings in the Good Food Guide.

By 1988, Judy and Michael had sold both Perry's and Barrenjoey House, and Michael became the General Manager of Bilson's in Circular Quay. Judy followed as the Function Manager, and together they ran that glittering icon until they purchased Catalina in 1994, transforming a tired old space into the perfect harbourside restaurant that it is today. In 2011 Michael was awarded the Vittoria Legend award at the Sydney Morning Herald Good Food Awards.

With this wonderful partnership as an example, the next generation of McMahons, James and Kate now also work in this family business.

James followed his parents into restaurants with stints at fine diners such as banc, Buon Ricordo and est., as well as Sydney favourites Rockpool Bar and Grill and China Doll. James now runs the restaurant and dining room and brings a wealth of fine dining and service experience to Catalina.

Kate came up through cocktail bars in the Merivale Group, such as Hemmesphere and The Den (at Ivy), and has brought in fresh and exciting ideas to Catalina. The greatest example of this is Kate's famed annual New Year's Day party, which has become an iconic event on Sydney’s party calendar. Kate runs the restaurant alongside James, and has also taken over much of the day to day administration.

Catalina has been a Sydney icon for the past 28 years with Michael and Judy at the helm. Judy, James and Kate continue to run Catalina, keeping Michael’s vision alive.

title: 14% off

merchant_editorial:
Diana is the brainchild of owner Sammy Jeon and is based on his cult-favourite regional karaoke bar, Mr Lim. Located in Orange, Mr Lim is one of four venues Jeon owns in regional NSW. After moving from a small Korean island to Griffith to pick apples, Jeon met winemakers Phillip and Diana Shaw. His time spent with the Shaw family sparked his love of food and wine, and has inspired the name of this new restaurant.

"When I moved to Orange, I worked in the vineyard at Philip Shaw wines and had dinner cooked by the family most days for two and a half years,"" Jeon said. ""The time spent with the Shaw family, and in particular Diana, was a very memorable experience and inspired the name for Diana Potts Point. Diana even came to visit me in Korea once, and although wasn't keen on the cuisine at first (she just wanted chips!), I managed to covert her through my cooking."

Jeon is now bringing the homely atmosphere of his regional outposts to the heart of Sydney, with Diana. On Wednesdays, Thursdays and Sundays, you can head to Diana for its combination of Korean and Chinese cuisine, sans karaoke. Throughout the week, you'll find standout dishes on the menu such as a sashimi salad that combines salmon, tuna, kingfish and abalone with a crisp collection of fresh vegetables; or twice-cooked spicy Korean fried chicken, seasoned with Korean chilli paste, spring onion and almonds.

name: Grace of India eGift Card
title: 14% off
subtitle: Enjoy 14% off
merchant_editorial:
Dear Diner,

Let me take you on journey pre Butter Chicken!

By the time the last Mughal was deposed by the British in 1858, Hindustan (India) was changed forever. Three centuries of Mughal reign left behind an enduring legacy of culinary art that permanently altered the landscape of Indian cuisine. The Mughals were lavish and extravagant, connoisseurs of beauty. Courtly food of the Mughal Empire was sumptuous, rich and complex. While the curries and gravies were cooked richer with creams and yogurt, the eyes feasted upon the decorated fresh flower petals and edible thin foils of silver and gold.

Mughlai cuisine then made a move overseas, post English Raj the English palate came to thirst for the ground spices of India and came the birth of the fusion of UK and Mughlai food. Popular dishes such as Chicken Tikka Masala, Butter Chicken and Balti came to the forefront during this period, a mix of Indian spices and English tomato and cream bases made this overwhelmingly popular, and so it remains today where the English have even made their national cuisine Indian food!

After three decades of cooking and serving, I hope to shed some light on the dishes pre and post Mughal Raj and not to forget my very own contemporary twists.

My family and I hope Grace of India takes you on a journey to the rivers, villages and cities of India through flavours, aroma and texture of our very best food.

Regards,

Santokh Singh

Head Chef and Owner

Though Kaiza Izakaya might look the part, the menu really does set it apart from your run-of-the-mill Newtown Japanese joint. Dip into Nguyen's polished take on izakaya favourites like silken tofu in ankake broth, a colourful sashimi platter, spicy pork udon, and gyu don with kombu dashi and teriyaki. But it'd be a crime to leave without trying Nguyen's more experimental fusion dishes. Like the wagyu nigiri with crispy rice, kizami wasabi, and cured egg yolk. It's a brilliant balance of flavours and surprising textures wrapped into one surprising parcel. The kitchen is a replica of a traditional South East Asian family home, lined with the smoking hot binchotan charcoal grill and piping hot woks. Kaiza is your home away from home, enjoy with friends and family, solo or for your next special occasion.

title: 14% off
subtitle: Enjoy 14% off
merchant_editorial:
Sicilian-born, award-winning chef Lino Sauro opened Olio Kensington Street in early 2017 to show Sydney why Sicilian is the new Italian. Olio Kensington Street (Italian for olive oil) is located in the beautifully restored The Old Rum Store with a contemporary indoor dining room plus lush rooftop terrace overlooking Chippendale.

Sauro comes from a long lineage of self-sufficient Sicilian farmers where he grew up with meals that were created with the quality produce grown on the property. This translates into Sauro’s menu with dishes that reflect a strong connection to his Sicilian roots.

Keeping it in the family, Sauro sources his olive oil – the base of all his dishes – from the family’s Sicilian farm. Typical of the region, seasonal seafood, wild fennel, raisins, saffron and couscous are hero ingredients on Olio Kensington Street’s menu.

For the past 10 years, Sauro has lived in Singapore heading up his first solo Sicilian restaurant venture showing locals that Italian food can be more than pizza and heavy, creamy sauces. Now it’s Sydney’s turn.

“This is the style of food my family and I eat in Sicily. At home we’re surrounded by the ocean and olive trees, so obviously olive oil and seafood are very important. Sicily’s historic past also mean there’s influences from North Africa and Mediterranean Europe. It’s a beautiful cuisine that I believe will translate really well to Australia given the location and climate here.” – Lino Sauro, Chef

Sefa a state of relaxed delight.

Our menu is influenced by an ancient region called Levantine. Levant is the Mediterranean part of the Middle East, covering Jordan, Lebanon, Israel, Palestine, Syria, Cyprus and the South East corner of Turkey. For the past 400 years the ancient cooking traditions of the area’s different ethnicities have mingled in this melting pot, creating a refined cuisine without national borders . The use of legumes, dairy, grains, spices and different cooking techniques, plus a strong focus on seasonal produce is inherent in this part of the world."" Sefa Kitchen is located at 292 Bondi Rd, Bondi, NSW.
